---
title: "From \\LaTeX{} to Quarto"
subtitle: "Scientific Writing, Reproducible Publishing, and Beyond"
author: "Imad El Badisy"
date: today
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: crane
    fonttheme: professionalfonts
    slide-level: 2
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Workshop Outline"
    toc-depth: 1
    number-sections: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    keep-tex: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{amsmath, amssymb}
        \usepackage{listings}
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        \usepackage{tikz}
        \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, shapes.geometric, positioning}
        \setbeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}
        \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{size=\normalsize, series=\bfseries}
        \definecolor{codeblue}{RGB}{30, 100, 200}
        \definecolor{codegreen}{RGB}{0, 130, 80}
        \lstset{
          basicstyle=\ttfamily\scriptsize,
          keywordstyle=\color{codeblue},
          breaklines=true,
          columns=fullflexible
        }
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: refs/references.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, eval = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.align = "center", out.width = "80%")
```


# Part 1 - Scientific Writing with \LaTeX{}

## 1. A Brief History

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="28%"}
![](figures/latex-logo.png){width=95%}
:::
::: {.column width="36%"}
**\TeX{} - D. Knuth, 1978**

- Underlying engine, still used today
- Deterministic, reproducible output

**\LaTeX{} - L. Lamport, 1984**

- Macro layer on top of \TeX{}
- Separates content from formatting
- Became the standard for scientific writing
:::
::: {.column width="36%"}
| Year | Event |
|---|---|
| 1978 | \TeX{} released |
| 1984 | \LaTeX{} 2.09 |
| 1994 | \LaTeX{}2e (current) |
| 1999 | pdf\TeX{} → direct PDF |
| 2004 | Xe\TeX{} → Unicode |
| 2007 | Lua\TeX{} → scripting |
| 2012 | Overleaf launched |
:::
:::

## 2. \LaTeX{} ≠ Overleaf

For many students, **Overleaf = LaTeX** - but they are not the same thing.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="36%"}
![](figures/latex-info.png){width=85%}
:::
::: {.column width="32%"}
**\LaTeX{} (1984)**

- A typesetting *language + compiler*
- Runs anywhere: machine, server, cloud
- Free and open source
- Works offline in any editor
:::
::: {.column width="32%"}
**Overleaf (2012)**

- A *web app* built on top of \LaTeX{}
- Runs the compiler on their servers
- Adds: browser UI, collaboration, cloud storage
- You are still writing \LaTeX{}
:::
:::

\medskip
**Analogy:** \LaTeX{} is the engine. Overleaf is the car. Swap the car - the engine stays the same.

## 3. Why \LaTeX{}?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The problem with Word**

- Copy-paste figures from R → manual update on every change
- `final_v3_FINAL_revised.docx`
- Math typesetting is painful

**\LaTeX{} advantages**

- Precise, professional typesetting
- Native math support
- Dominant in stats / math / physics journals
- Plain text → version control with Git
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
| Feature | Word | \LaTeX{} |
|---|---|---|
| Typesetting | Medium | Excellent |
| Math | Poor | Native |
| Figure placement | Manual | Algorithmic |
| Bibliography | Plugin | Built-in |
| Version control | Hard | Easy |
| Journal templates | Limited | Extensive |

**Use \LaTeX{} for:** thesis, journal articles, technical reports with equations.
:::
:::

## 4. Where to Write \LaTeX{} - Platforms

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
| Platform | Type | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **Prism** (OpenAI) | Browser | Beginners + AI writing |
| **Overleaf** | Browser | Collaboration |
| **TeXStudio** | Desktop | Full-featured editor |
| **VS Code** + LaTeX Workshop | Desktop | Programmers / Git |
| **TeXmaker** | Desktop | Lightweight editor |
| **RStudio** | Desktop | R users |

\medskip
**Recommendation:** beginners → Prism or Overleaf; advanced → VS Code + Git.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/prism-screenshot.png){width=100%}

\small Free \LaTeX{} workspace with GPT-5.2 integrated for writing and debugging.
:::
:::

## 5. Why Document Classes?

`\documentclass` is the first decision - it sets the layout, section hierarchy, and all defaults.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
| Class | Use |
|---|---|
| `article` | Papers, short reports |
| `report` | Theses, longer reports |
| `book` | Books, front/back matter |
| `beamer` | Slides |
| `elsarticle` | Elsevier journals |
| `IEEEtran` | IEEE journals |

**Why it matters:** `\section{}` renders differently across classes. Always start from the journal's `.cls` file.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```latex
% Generic paper
\documentclass[12pt]{article}

% Two-column journal
\documentclass[twocolumn]{article}

% Elsevier submission
\documentclass{elsarticle}

% Slide deck
\documentclass{beamer}
\usetheme{Madrid}
```

The class file controls everything - margins, fonts, title format, header/footer.
:::
:::

## 6. Document Structure

```latex
\documentclass[12pt, a4paper]{article}   % (1) class

% (2) Preamble - packages and settings
\usepackage{amsmath, amssymb}   % math
\usepackage{graphicx}           % figures
\usepackage{booktabs}           % tables
\usepackage{hyperref}           % links
\usepackage[style=apa, backend=biber]{biblatex}
\addbibresource{references.bib}

\title{My Paper}
\author{Author Name}
\date{\today}

% (3) Body
\begin{document}
\maketitle
\tableofcontents

\section{Introduction}
Text here. Cite with \parencite{cox1972}.

\printbibliography
\end{document}
```

## 7. How to Compile

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](figures/latex-engines.png){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
```bash
# pdflatex (most common)
pdflatex paper.tex
biber paper        # bibliography
pdflatex paper.tex # 2nd pass: refs

# xelatex (Unicode + fonts)
xelatex paper.tex

# latexmk (recommended)
# automates all passes
latexmk -xelatex paper.tex
```

\medskip
**Run at least twice** - cross-references are resolved on the second pass.

**Overleaf** handles this automatically.
:::
:::

## 8. Mathematical Notation

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Inline:** `$\hat{\beta} = 0.42$` → $\hat{\beta} = 0.42$

**Display:**
```latex
\begin{equation}
  \hat{\beta} = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y
\end{equation}
```

**Aligned:**
```latex
\begin{align}
  \ell(\theta) &= \sum_{i=1}^n \log f(y_i \mid \theta) \\
  \hat{\theta} &= \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta)
\end{align}
```

**Custom commands** (define once):
```latex
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\Var}{\mathrm{Var}}
\newcommand{\iid}{\overset{iid}{\sim}}
```
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
| Symbol | \LaTeX{} |
|---|---|
| $\sum$ | `\sum` |
| $\prod$ | `\prod` |
| $\int$ | `\int` |
| $\hat{\theta}$ | `\hat{\theta}` |
| $\alpha, \beta$ | `\alpha, \beta` |
| $\mathbb{E}[X]$ | `\mathbb{E}[X]` |
| $\leq, \geq$ | `\leq, \geq` |
| $\in, \notin$ | `\in, \notin` |
| $\sim$ | `\sim` |
| $X^\top$ | `X^\top` |

**Demo →** `demos/latex/02-math/`
:::
:::

## 9. Diagrams - TikZ and Alternatives

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**TikZ** - standard \LaTeX{} diagram package

```latex
\usepackage{tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning}

\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1cm,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners,
    fill=blue!8, minimum width=2.8cm}]
  \node[box] (A) {Inclusion};
  \node[box, below=of A] (B) {Eligible};
  \node[box, below=of B] (C) {Analysis};
  \draw[->] (A)--(B); \draw[->] (B)--(C);
\end{tikzpicture}
```

Other uses: DAGs, survival trees, neural nets, timelines.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Alternatives in R / Quarto**

| Tool | Best for |
|---|---|
| **DiagrammeR** | Flowcharts, networks |
| **ggdag** | Causal DAGs |
| **ggraph** | Graph visualization |
| **Mermaid** | Quick diagrams in `.qmd` |

**Mermaid - no package needed:**

````markdown
```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Write] --> B{Error?}
  B -- yes --> C[Fix]
  C --> B
  B -- no --> D[Output]
```
````
:::
:::

## 10. Debugging with AI Coding Agents

Modern AI agents (Claude, Codex, GitHub Copilot, Cursor) accelerate \LaTeX{} and Quarto debugging.

**What to give the agent:**

1. The **exact error message** from the terminal or log
2. The **minimal code block** that triggers the error
3. The **context**: document class, relevant packages, what you expected

**Effective prompt:**

```
I am writing LaTeX with \documentclass{article} and \usepackage{amsmath}.
The following snippet fails to compile:

  \begin{align}
    y = x^2
  \end{align*}

Error: "Extra \begin{align}."
What is wrong and how do I fix it?
```

**Key rule:** never paste the entire document - isolate the failing block first.

## 11. AI-Assisted Debugging Workflow

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node distance=0.6cm,
  box/.style={
    rectangle, rounded corners=4pt,
    draw=gray!70, fill=blue!7,
    minimum width=4.2cm, minimum height=0.65cm,
    align=center, font=\small
  },
  dec/.style={
    diamond, aspect=2.8,
    draw=orange!80, fill=orange!12,
    align=center, font=\small, inner sep=2pt
  },
  arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=5pt]}, thick, gray!60}
]
\node[box]  (write)   {Write \LaTeX{} / Quarto};
\node[box,  below=of write]   (compile) {Compile};
\node[dec,  below=of compile] (err)     {Error?};
\node[box,  below=of err]     (copy)    {Copy error + minimal snippet};
\node[box,  below=of copy]    (agent)   {AI Agent (Claude / Codex / Copilot)};
\node[box,  below=of agent]   (fix)     {Apply suggested fix};
\node[box,  right=2.4cm of err] (out)   {Final Output};
\draw[arr] (write)   -- (compile);
\draw[arr] (compile) -- (err);
\draw[arr] (err)     -- node[left,  font=\footnotesize]{yes} (copy);
\draw[arr] (copy)    -- (agent);
\draw[arr] (agent)   -- (fix);
\draw[arr] (fix.west) -- ++(-1.3,0) |- (compile.west);
\draw[arr] (err)     -- node[above, font=\footnotesize]{no}  (out);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2em}
{\small\textbf{Demo →} \texttt{demos/latex/} - 01-minimal, 02-math, 03-template, 04-bibliography}
```


# Part 2 - Quarto for Reproducible Publishing

## 12. From \LaTeX{} to Quarto - Why?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
| \LaTeX{} | Quarto |
|---|---|
| Excellent typesetting | Same \LaTeX{} engine underneath |
| Manual copy-paste of results | Results computed inside the document |
| One output format | HTML, PDF, slides, website, book |
| Text only | Code + text unified |
| Steep learning curve | Markdown + YAML + code |

\medskip
**Quarto = \LaTeX{} quality + reproducible code + multiple outputs from one source**
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](figures/rmarkdown-logo.png){width=44%}\ ![](figures/quarto-logo.png){width=44%}

\small R Markdown (2012) was the predecessor. Quarto (2022) is the successor - multi-language, multi-output.
:::
:::

## 13. What Quarto Solves

Quarto turns one plain-text `.qmd` source into many publishable outputs.

| Need | Output format |
|---|---|
| Web report / dashboard | HTML |
| Submission / print | PDF (via \LaTeX{}) |
| Lecture slides | RevealJS (browser) or Beamer (PDF) |
| Course or research site | Website |
| Thesis / textbook | Book → HTML + PDF + EPUB |
| Repeated journal format | Template / extension |

**One source file → many targets. No copy-paste.**

## 14. Engines, Compilers and Install

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Render pipeline:**

```
.qmd → knitr (R) ──┐
                   ├→ .md → Pandoc → HTML / docx
.qmd → jupyter ───┘              └→ xelatex → PDF
```

| Layer | Tool | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Computation | `knitr` / `jupyter` | Run code, embed output |
| Markdown → HTML | Pandoc | Convert markup |
| Markdown → PDF | `xelatex` | Compile \LaTeX{} |
| Orchestrator | Quarto CLI | Ties all layers |
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Install check:**

```bash
quarto --version
quarto check       # diagnose engines
```

**Render commands:**

```bash
quarto render report.qmd
quarto render report.qmd --to pdf
quarto render report.qmd --to html
quarto preview report.qmd  # live
```

`quarto check` shows which engines are installed and working.
:::
:::

## 15. File Anatomy

Every `.qmd` file has three layers:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```yaml
---
title: "My Report"        # YAML
author: "Student Name"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
  pdf:
    number-sections: true
execute:
  warning: false
bibliography: refs.bib
---
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```markdown
## Introduction    ← Markdown body

This is **bold** and *italic*.

- First item
- Second item
```

````markdown
```{r}             ← Code chunk
#| label: fig-km
#| fig-cap: "KM curve."
survfit(...) |> plot()
```
````

Rule: write once → render to any format.
:::
:::

## 16. Markdown - The Prose Layer

**Markdown** (J. Gruber, 2004) - lightweight markup, readable as plain text, converts to HTML/PDF via Pandoc.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Relation to \LaTeX{}**

| Aspect | \LaTeX{} | Markdown |
|---|---|---|
| Design goal | Precision | Readability |
| Math | Native | Via \LaTeX{} engine |
| Output | PDF mainly | Many (via Pandoc) |
| Verbosity | High | Minimal |

In Quarto: Markdown is the prose layer. \LaTeX{} runs underneath for PDF.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Syntax comparison**

| Element | \LaTeX{} | Markdown |
|---|---|---|
| Bold | `\textbf{x}` | `**x**` |
| Italic | `\textit{x}` | `*x*` |
| Section | `\section{T}` | `## T` |
| Bullet | `\begin{itemize}` | `- item` |
| Link | `\href{u}{t}` | `[t](u)` |
| Code | `\texttt{x}` | `` `x` `` |
| Image | `\includegraphics` | `![](path)` |
:::
:::

## 17. YAML - The Control Layer

YAML controls the entire output without touching the content.

```yaml
---
title: "Survival Analysis Report"
author: "Imad El Badisy"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---
```

Change the `format` block → completely different output, same source. Add a format → get a new output for free.

## 18. Code Chunks and Inline Code

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Chunk options** (`#|` syntax - same for R, Python, Julia):

| Option | Effect |
|---|---|
| `label` | names output for `@fig-` refs |
| `echo` | show / hide source code |
| `eval` | run / skip chunk |
| `fig-cap` | figure caption |
| `tbl-cap` | table caption |
| `warning` | show / hide warnings |

````markdown
```{r}
#| label: fig-km
#| fig-cap: "Kaplan-Meier curve."
#| echo: false
survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = df) |>
  plot()
```
````
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Inline code** - live values in prose:

```markdown
The dataset has `r nrow(mtcars)` rows.
Mean MPG: `r round(mean(mtcars$mpg), 1)`.
```

Numbers update automatically when the data changes - no manual editing.

\medskip
**Demo →** `demos/quarto/01-minimal/`
:::
:::

## 19. Cross-References and Citations

**Figures, tables, equations** - use label prefixes:

```markdown
![Flow diagram](flow.png){#fig-flow}    → cite as @fig-flow
: Patient groups {#tbl-groups}          → cite as @tbl-groups
$$ h(t|x) = h_0(t)e^{x^\top\beta} $$ {#eq-cox}  → @eq-cox
```

**Citations** - add `.bib` in YAML, then cite inline:

```yaml
bibliography: references.bib
```

```markdown
The Cox model [@cox1972] is widely used in survival analysis.
```

**Label prefixes are mandatory:** `fig-`, `tbl-`, `eq-`, `sec-` - wrong prefix = broken reference.

**Demo →** `demos/quarto/02-crossrefs/`

## 20. Output Formats

| Format | YAML key | Render command | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTML | `html` | `--to html` | Web, interactive |
| PDF | `pdf` | `--to pdf` | Submission, archiving |
| Word | `docx` | `--to docx` | Collaborators |
| Beamer slides | `beamer` | `--to beamer` | PDF lectures |
| RevealJS slides | `revealjs` | `--to revealjs` | Browser talks |
| Website | project `website` | `quarto preview` | Course / research site |
| Book | project `book` | `quarto render` | Thesis, textbook |
| EPUB | `epub` | `--to epub` | Mobile reading |

All formats share one source - switching is a YAML change, not a rewrite.

## 21. Slides - RevealJS vs Beamer

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**RevealJS** - browser slides

```yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
```

- Runs in any browser
- Animations, fragments, speaker notes
- Easy to share: just a URL
- Interactive elements (Observable)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Beamer** - PDF slides

```yaml
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: crane
    pdf-engine: xelatex
```

- Classic conference / print format
- Full \LaTeX{} math and TikZ
- Works offline, always renders the same
:::
:::

**Structure:** with `slide-level: 2`, `#` headings → sections, `##` headings → slides.

**Demo →** `demos/quarto/03-slides/` and `demos/quarto-lecture/02-slides/`

## 22. Projects and `_quarto.yml`

Use a **project** when you have more than one file.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
```text
my-project/
  _quarto.yml
  index.qmd
  methods.qmd
  results.qmd
  references.bib
  figures/
```

```bash
quarto render    # all files
quarto preview   # live reload
quarto create project book my-book
quarto create project website my-site
```
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
```yaml
# _quarto.yml - shared config
project:
  type: website     # or: book, default
  output-dir: _output

website:
  title: "My Course"
  navbar:
    left:
      - index.qmd
      - methods.qmd

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    number-sections: true
```
:::
:::

**Demo →** `demos/quarto-lecture/` - 01-report, 02-slides, 03-website, 04-book, 05-template

## 23. Templates

Use templates when the same structure repeats across documents.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Apply a template:**

```bash
quarto use template owner/repository
```

**Workflow:**

1. Render unchanged - verify it compiles
2. Edit YAML (title, author, date)
3. Render again
4. Replace body content
5. Final render

**Useful repos:**

- `quarto-journals` - journal formats
- `quarto-thesis` - thesis template
- `quarto-revealjs-clean` - clean slides
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Create your own template:**

```text
my-template/
  template.qmd     ← example document
  _extensions/
    my-template/
      _extension.yml
      custom.tex    ← LaTeX preamble
      theme.scss    ← HTML theme
```

A template is just a `.qmd` file with a custom format - share it with `quarto use template`.

**Demo →** `demos/quarto-lecture/05-template/`
:::
:::

## 24. Debugging Checklist

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
| Problem | Where to look |
|---|---|
| YAML fails | indentation - 2 spaces, no tabs |
| Figure missing | relative path from `.qmd` |
| Cross-ref broken | correct prefix: `fig-`, `tbl-`, `eq-` |
| PDF fails | run `quarto check`; LaTeX engine |
| Citation missing | `.bib` path + exact cite key |
| Code chunk fails | engine installed, package loaded |

**HTML works but PDF fails?**

```bash
quarto render report.qmd --to html  # isolate
quarto render report.qmd --to pdf
```

Raw HTML (`<div>`, widgets) breaks PDF output.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Key commands:**

```bash
quarto --version
quarto check
quarto render report.qmd
quarto render report.qmd --to pdf
quarto preview report.qmd
quarto create project book my-book
quarto create project website my-site
quarto use template owner/repo
```

**When stuck:** use an AI agent with the exact error + minimal snippet (see slide 10).
:::
:::

## 25. Lab - Build Your Own Templates

**3 hours remaining - build from scratch in pairs or solo:**

| Task | Output | Key concepts |
|---|---|---|
| **1** - Minimal report | HTML + PDF | YAML, Markdown, code chunk |
| **2** - Slide deck | RevealJS or Beamer | `slide-level`, themes |
| **3** - Website | HTML site | `_quarto.yml`, navbar |
| **4** - Book / thesis chapter | HTML + PDF | chapters, cross-refs |
| **5** - eBook | EPUB | `epub` format, covers |
| **6** - Custom template | reusable format | `_extensions/`, custom \LaTeX{} |

\medskip
**Start from `demos/quarto-lecture/` - one folder per task.**

Each output must have: a title page, one figure with caption and cross-reference, one equation, and a bibliography entry.
